description: Question-led source-of-truth model for a GreenOps data input maturity assessment and reporting tool. Designed for derived scoring rather than direct self-selected maturity.

1.label: Initial / Ad hoc
1.description: Inputs are fragmented, reactive, estimate-heavy and not reliable enough for confident decision-making.

2.label: Managed / Repeatable
2.description: Inputs can be gathered and reused in a repeatable way, but coverage, precision and attribution remain inconsistent.

3.label: Defined / Standardised
3.description: Data sources, methods and ownership are documented and standardised across key areas.

4.label: Quantitatively Managed / Measured
4.description: Data quality, granularity, coverage and confidence are measured and controlled using evidence.

5.label: Optimising
5.description: Inputs are continuously improved, increasingly automated and embedded into operational and strategic decision-making.

1.label: Reporting only
1.description: Data is suitable mainly for broad directional reporting and high-level awareness.

2.label: Directional decision support
2.description: Data can support indicative prioritisation but not high-confidence operational decisions.

3.label: Controlled decision-grade
3.description: Data is sufficiently reliable for controlled operational and management decisions in defined areas.

4.label: Optimisation-grade
4.description: Data is sufficiently granular, timely and attributable to support continuous optimisation and stronger automation.

global_scoring_rules.primary_principle: Domain maturity is calculated from weighted question responses, not directly selected by the user as the primary assessment method.
global_scoring_rules.derived_score_logic: Calculate weighted average score per domain using question weights. Convert to integer maturity band using nearest band with upward rounding from .75.
global_scoring_rules.direct_manual_override_policy: If manual override is allowed, it must be optional, secondary, clearly labelled as assessor override, and the tool must preserve both calculated and overridden scores.
global_scoring_rules.confidence_guidance: Confidence should reflect evidence quality, consistency of answers, clarity of lineage and completeness of rationale.
global_scoring_rules.impact_guidance: Impact is a separate score reflecting materiality, decision relevance, dependency value and risk if weak.

domains.name: Asset inventory and configuration data
domains.definition: Assesses whether the technology estate is known well enough to support GreenOps calculations across cloud, on-premise, end-user and colocation environments.
domains.why_it_matters: Weak inventory data undermines embodied emissions, service mapping, lifecycle decisions, utilisation analysis and attribution.
recommendation_themes: Link assets to services, owners and lifecycle state

scoring.normalisation: weighted average of selected option scores (1-5), rounded to nearest whole number with .75 thresholds upward
question_override_rules: If more than 30% of answers are level 1, overall maturity cannot exceed 3 unless assessor override is applied.
If evidence coverage is weak across more than half of weighted questions, confidence should be reduced.
If assurance/lineage questions score 1 or 2, decision-readiness should be capped at directional support unless other evidence is strong and override is used.

confidence_logic.low: Sparse evidence, inconsistent notes, or material contradictions across answers.
confidence_logic.medium: Reasonable evidence coverage with some gaps or assumptions.
confidence_logic.high: Strong evidence coverage, consistent answers, clear ownership, documented methods.

questions.text: Do you have a reliable inventory of IT assets across all your environments?
questions.evidence_hint: Inventory coverage report, asset counts by domain

options.label: Major gaps. We do not have a reliable view across the full estate.
We cover some key areas, but there are clear gaps or inconsistencies.
We have a usable inventory across most main environments.
We have good coverage and actively check data quality.
We have strong, trusted coverage across the estate and keep it up to date.

questions.text: How detailed is the asset data you hold?

options.label: Very limited. We often only know the basics, if that.
Some useful fields exist, but data is patchy and inconsistent.
We hold the main fields needed for most assets.
We have good-quality detailed data across most of the estate.
We have rich, trusted asset data that supports operational and GreenOps decisions.

questions.text: Can you link assets to the services, applications, platforms or teams that use them?

options.label: Rarely. Most assets are not meaningfully linked.
Some links exist, but only for certain platforms or teams.
We can link many key assets to known owners or services.
We can link most relevant assets to services, platforms or teams.
We have strong service and ownership mapping across the estate.

questions.text: How quickly does the inventory reflect changes such as deployments, moves or removals?

options.label: It is often out of date.
Updates happen, but not reliably or quickly.
It is usually updated in a reasonable timeframe.
It is kept up to date across most key environments.
Changes are reflected quickly and consistently as part of normal operations.

questions.text: Can you tell where assets are actually deployed?

options.label: Not reliably.
Only at a broad level for some areas.
We can usually place assets by key environment or location.
We can locate assets well across most of the estate.
We can pinpoint assets at the level needed for real operational and GreenOps use.

questions.text: How easy is it to match your inventory to product data needed for GreenOps calculations?

options.label: Very difficult. Matching is mostly manual or guesswork.
We can match some assets, but with lots of effort and inconsistency.
We can match many core assets reasonably well.
Matching works well across most of the relevant estate.
Matching is strong, efficient and reliable across the main asset base.

questions.text: Can you explain where your inventory data comes from and how trustworthy it is?

options.label: Not clearly.
We know some main sources, but trust varies a lot.
We can explain the main sources and trust them reasonably well.
Source systems and trust levels are clear across most key areas.
Lineage and confidence are clear, understood and actively managed.

questions.text: Do you track lifecycle status properly, for example in use, spare, due for refresh, retired or awaiting disposal?

options.label: Hardly at all.
We track some lifecycle states, but not consistently.
We have a workable lifecycle view for most main assets.
Lifecycle tracking is strong across most of the estate.
Lifecycle status is clear, trusted and supports refresh and circularity decisions.

1.label: Initial / Ad hoc
1.description: Inputs are fragmented, reactive, estimate-heavy and not reliable enough for confident decision-making.

2.label: Managed / Repeatable
2.description: Inputs can be gathered and reused in a repeatable way, but coverage, precision and attribution remain inconsistent.

3.label: Defined / Standardised
3.description: Data sources, methods and ownership are documented and standardised across key areas.

4.label: Quantitatively Managed / Measured
4.description: Data quality, granularity, coverage and confidence are measured and controlled using evidence.

5.label: Optimising
5.description: Inputs are continuously improved, increasingly automated and embedded into operational and strategic decision-making.

supports: Broad awareness and high-level reporting for asset inventory and configuration data

does_not_support: Confident automation or tight target accountability

supports: Some controlled decisions in priority areas
does_not_support: Strong claims of precision across the whole estate

supports: Strong management, engineering and governance use
does_not_support: None by design; caveats should still be made explicit where relevant

recommendation_triggers.guidance: Prioritise foundational improvement in asset inventory and configuration data before making strong decision-grade claims.
Standardise and expand asset inventory and configuration data so it supports more controlled decisions.
Use stronger asset inventory and configuration data capability to drive optimisation and measurable intervention, not just reporting.

domains.name: Operational power and energy data
domains.definition: Assesses the quality, granularity and usability of operational electricity and energy inputs across on-premise, colocation and, where possible, cloud-related models.
domains.why_it_matters: Energy is the core activity driver for many GreenOps calculations. Weak energy data limits reporting, overhead allocation, optimisation and hotspot detection.
recommendation_themes: Replace spend-based estimates with measured energy
Increase granularity from site to rack/host/workload where justified
Strengthen attribution to services and workloads

scoring.normalisation: weighted average of selected option scores (1-5), rounded to nearest whole number with .75 thresholds upward
question_override_rules: If more than 30% of answers are level 1, overall maturity cannot exceed 3 unless assessor override is applied.
If evidence coverage is weak across more than half of weighted questions, confidence should be reduced.
If assurance/lineage questions score 1 or 2, decision-readiness should be capped at directional support unless other evidence is strong and override is used.

confidence_logic.low: Sparse evidence, inconsistent notes, or material contradictions across answers.
confidence_logic.medium: Reasonable evidence coverage with some gaps or assumptions.
confidence_logic.high: Strong evidence coverage, consistent answers, clear ownership, documented methods.

questions.text: What do you mainly use today to work out operational energy use for on-premise and colocation environments?

options.label: Mostly estimates based on spend, contracts, floor space or broad assumptions.
Site or provider totals with simple allocation rules.
Measured site-level energy data for most key environments.
Measured data at a more detailed level, such as hall, rack or cluster.
Granular, trusted energy data linked to actual platforms, services or workloads.

questions.text: How detailed is your energy data in practice?

options.label: Very broad only.
Broad totals with limited breakdown.
Good enough for major sites or environments.
Detailed across most operationally important areas.
Detailed enough to support targeted optimisation and attribution.

questions.text: How much of the estate is covered by measured or modelled energy data?

options.label: Very little.
Some important areas only.
Most main environments are covered.
Coverage is strong across most of the relevant estate.
Coverage is broad, trusted and actively monitored.

questions.text: Can you link energy use back to services, platforms, teams or business demand?

options.label: Not really.
Only at a very broad level.
We can make some useful links for major areas.
We can link energy to many operational owners or services.
We can link energy meaningfully to the level where action can be taken.

questions.text: How often is energy data available at a useful level?

options.label: Irregularly or only for reporting exercises.
Periodically, but not often enough for much operational use.
Often enough for baseline tracking and some decisions.
Frequent enough for regular operational use.
Timely enough for ongoing optimisation where needed.

questions.text: How quickly can you get the data after the energy has been used?

options.label: Very slow.
Slow enough to limit usefulness.
Reasonable for most reporting and planning needs.
Fast enough for regular management use.
Fast enough to support near-operational action where needed.

questions.text: Does the data reflect where the energy was actually used?

options.label: Hardly at all.
Only at broad site or provider level.
Generally yes for major environments.
Yes across most of the places that matter.
Yes, with strong location accuracy for real decision-making.

questions.text: Can you show clearly where the energy data came from and how it was calculated?

options.label: Not clearly.
Partly, but there are gaps.
The main source and method are clear.
Source and method are clear across most relevant areas.
Source, method and confidence are transparent and trusted.

questions.text: How confident are you in the accuracy and calibration of your energy measurement infrastructure — meters, sensors, and data collection systems?
questions.guidance: Measured energy data is only as good as the measurement infrastructure. Sub-metering quality, calibration frequency, and data collection reliability directly affect the credibility of all downstream calculations.
questions.evidence_hint: Meter calibration records, sub-metering architecture documentation, data quality audit results

options.label: We have no visibility into meter quality or calibration status.
We know where meters are but calibration is irregular and data gaps are not tracked.
Major meters are identified and periodically calibrated. We can identify data gaps but remediation is ad-hoc.
Meter infrastructure is documented, calibrated on schedule, and data gaps are tracked and escalated. Sub-metering covers key zones.
Full measurement assurance programme with documented calibration, automated gap detection, sub-metering at equipment or rack level, and regular data quality audits.

domains.name: Water data
domains.definition: Assesses the quality, applicability and context of water inputs used in GreenOps calculations, particularly for data centre, cooling and AI-relevant workloads.
domains.why_it_matters: Water is often under-measured yet increasingly material. Weak water data limits reporting, site strategy, cooling assessment and environmental trade-off decisions.
recommendation_themes: Establish basic water reporting where material
Link water to cooling modes and site context
Bring in local water stress context

scoring.normalisation: weighted average of selected option scores (1-5), rounded to nearest whole number with .75 thresholds upward
question_override_rules: If more than 30% of answers are level 1, overall maturity cannot exceed 3 unless assessor override is applied.
If evidence coverage is weak across more than half of weighted questions, confidence should be reduced.
If assurance/lineage questions score 1 or 2, decision-readiness should be capped at directional support unless other evidence is strong and override is used.

confidence_logic.low: Sparse evidence, inconsistent notes, or material contradictions across answers.
confidence_logic.medium: Reasonable evidence coverage with some gaps or assumptions.
confidence_logic.high: Strong evidence coverage, consistent answers, clear ownership, documented methods.

questions.text: Do you have water consumption data for the facilities or providers that matter to your GreenOps calculations?

options.label: No, or almost none.
Limited data from some sites or providers.
A usable baseline for the main facilities that matter.
Good-quality water data across most relevant environments.
Strong, trusted water data that supports real decisions.

questions.text: How detailed is the water data you receive or collect?

options.label: Very broad only.
Some totals, but little useful breakdown.
Enough detail for baseline reporting and some analysis.
Good detail across most relevant sites or providers.
Detailed enough to support operational and location-specific decisions.

questions.text: Can you link water use meaningfully to cooling or IT activity?

options.label: Not really.
Only with broad assumptions.
We can make a reasonable link for key environments.
We can link water use well across most relevant areas.
We can link it credibly enough to support decisions and trade-offs.

questions.text: How often is water data available at a useful level?

options.label: Rarely.
Periodically, but quite limited.
Regular enough for baseline tracking.
Frequent enough for regular review.
Timely enough to support active management where needed.

questions.text: Does the water data reflect where it is being used and the local context?

options.label: No meaningful location context.
Limited location context only.
Some useful location context for main sites.
Good location context across most relevant environments.
Strong location context, including where local conditions materially matter.

questions.text: How quickly can you access water data after the usage happens?

options.label: Very slowly.
Slow enough to reduce usefulness.
Reasonable for most reporting needs.
Good enough for regular management.
Fast enough for practical decision support.

questions.text: How strong is the method behind your water calculations?

options.label: Weak or unclear.
Basic and heavily assumption-led.
Reasonable and repeatable for the main use cases.
Strong and consistent across most relevant areas.
Robust enough for credible decision-making.

questions.text: Can you explain clearly where the water data came from and how much confidence you have in it?

options.label: Not clearly.
Only partly.
The main source and confidence level are reasonably clear.
Source and confidence are clear across most relevant areas.
Source, method and confidence are transparent and trusted.

domains.name: Infrastructure efficiency metric quality
domains.definition: Assesses the quality and applicability of PUE, WUE, CUE, CER, CLF and similar metrics used to translate IT activity into total infrastructure impact.
domains.why_it_matters: Weak efficiency metrics can distort non-IT load allocation and make outputs look more precise than they really are.
recommendation_themes: Replace design or marketing values with operational values

scoring.normalisation: weighted average of selected option scores (1-5), rounded to nearest whole number with .75 thresholds upward
question_override_rules: If more than 30% of answers are level 1, overall maturity cannot exceed 3 unless assessor override is applied.
If evidence coverage is weak across more than half of weighted questions, confidence should be reduced.
If assurance/lineage questions score 1 or 2, decision-readiness should be capped at directional support unless other evidence is strong and override is used.

confidence_logic.low: Sparse evidence, inconsistent notes, or material contradictions across answers.
confidence_logic.medium: Reasonable evidence coverage with some gaps or assumptions.
confidence_logic.high: Strong evidence coverage, consistent answers, clear ownership, documented methods.

questions.text: What is the main basis for metrics like PUE, WUE, CUE and similar measures used in your calculations?

options.label: Assumed values, design figures or provider marketing claims.
Broad site or provider values that are repeatable but generic.
Documented values with reasonably clear boundaries.
Measured operational values reviewed over time.
Well-defined, trusted metrics fit for real decision-making.

questions.text: Are the boundaries for these metrics clearly defined and used consistently?

options.label: No.
Only partly.
Yes for the main areas, with some gaps.
Yes across most relevant environments.
Yes, very clearly and consistently.

questions.text: Do the values reflect real operating conditions?

options.label: Not really.
Only loosely.
Broadly yes for the main areas.
Yes across most operationally important areas.
Yes, including changing conditions where that matters.

questions.text: Are these metrics detailed enough for where you are applying them?

options.label: No, they are too broad.
Only for high-level reporting.
Good enough for some practical use.
Good enough for most intended uses.
Detailed enough for targeted and credible allocation.

questions.text: Do they reflect the actual site, facility, hall or environment they relate to?

options.label: Rarely.
Only at a broad level.
Usually for the main sites or environments.
Yes across most relevant cases.
Yes, with strong location and boundary accuracy.

questions.text: How often are these metrics refreshed or checked?

options.label: Hardly ever.
Infrequently.
Periodically and reasonably consistently.
Regularly enough for management and review.
Frequently enough to stay decision-relevant.

questions.text: How confident are you that these metrics support credible allocation of non-IT overhead?

options.label: Low confidence.
Some confidence, but major caveats.
Reasonable confidence for broad use.
Good confidence for most intended decisions.
Strong confidence for real operational and GreenOps use.

questions.text: Can you show where these metrics came from and how they were derived?

options.label: Not clearly.
Only partly.
The main source and logic are clear.
The derivation is clear across most relevant cases.
The metrics are transparent, traceable and well evidenced.

domains.name: Embodied emissions data
domains.definition: Assesses the specificity, reliability and usefulness of embodied emissions inputs used for lifecycle, procurement, refresh and circularity decisions.
domains.why_it_matters: Embodied emissions are increasingly material. Weak inputs lead to poor refresh assumptions and weak supplier challenge.
recommendation_themes: Support reuse and refresh extension decisions

scoring.normalisation: weighted average of selected option scores (1-5), rounded to nearest whole number with .75 thresholds upward
question_override_rules: If more than 30% of answers are level 1, overall maturity cannot exceed 3 unless assessor override is applied.
If evidence coverage is weak across more than half of weighted questions, confidence should be reduced.
If assurance/lineage questions score 1 or 2, decision-readiness should be capped at directional support unless other evidence is strong and override is used.

confidence_logic.low: Sparse evidence, inconsistent notes, or material contradictions across answers.
confidence_logic.medium: Reasonable evidence coverage with some gaps or assumptions.
confidence_logic.high: Strong evidence coverage, consistent answers, clear ownership, documented methods.

questions.text: What do you mainly use today to estimate embodied emissions?

options.label: We do not really have embodied data, or we use spend-based factors only.
Broad category averages.
Product-family or supplier-level averages for most key areas.
Product- or supplier-specific data in many cases.
High-quality product-level lifecycle data that supports real decisions.

questions.text: How specific is that embodied data to the actual products and assets you use?

options.label: Not very specific at all.
Only loosely matched.
Reasonably specific for many key assets.
Specific across most of the relevant estate.
Highly specific and trusted at product level.

questions.text: How much of your estate can you match to embodied emissions data with reasonable confidence?

options.label: Very little.
Some core areas only.
A usable portion of the main estate.
Most relevant assets can be matched well.
Matching coverage is strong and actively managed.

questions.text: Do you link embodied data to real lifecycle context such as age, refresh plans, reuse and end of life?

options.label: Not really.
Only in limited cases.
For some important asset groups.
For most relevant assets and decisions.
Yes, as part of normal lifecycle and circularity management.

questions.text: Can you reflect supplier-specific or product-specific differences where they matter?

options.label: No.
Only occasionally.
In some key cases.
In most relevant cases.
Yes, consistently where it affects decisions.

questions.text: Is the embodied dataset current enough to support procurement and refresh decisions?

options.label: No.
Only loosely.
Broadly yes for key decisions.
Yes for most relevant decisions.
Yes, and it is actively maintained for decision use.

questions.text: Can you explain where the embodied values came from and how strong the evidence is?

options.label: Not clearly.
Only at a basic level.
The main basis is reasonably clear.
Sources and evidence quality are clear across most cases.
The values are well evidenced, transparent and trusted.

questions.text: Is the embodied data good enough to support real decisions, or only broad reporting?

options.label: Only broad reporting, if that.
Mostly rough directional insight.
Good enough for some practical decisions.
Good enough for most refresh and procurement decisions.
Clearly decision-grade for lifecycle, supplier and circularity choices.

questions.text: For major hardware suppliers, do you have visibility into their manufacturing carbon intensity? Is this factored into supplier selection?
questions.guidance: Supplier carbon intensity varies dramatically. Many major vendors now publish Scope 1+2 emissions data. Using this in procurement decisions is a material lever for reducing embodied carbon.
questions.evidence_hint: Supplier environmental disclosures, procurement evaluation criteria, supplier scorecards

options.label: No visibility. We select suppliers on cost and specification only.
Some suppliers publish data but we do not use it in procurement decisions.
We have carbon intensity data for major suppliers. Sometimes considered in selection.
Supplier carbon intensity is part of formal procurement criteria and actively incorporated into decisions.
Supplier carbon data is integrated into embodied carbon calculations. Cost versus carbon intensity trade-offs are explicit in procurement.

questions.text: In refresh and procurement cycles, do you systematically evaluate circular options such as refurbished, remanufactured, or extended-life alternatives?
questions.guidance: Circular alternatives can dramatically reduce embodied carbon. This requires explicit evaluation and quantified trade-off analysis rather than defaulting to new hardware procurement.
questions.evidence_hint: Procurement policies on refurbished hardware, embodied carbon comparisons, circular inventory tracking

options.label: No. We procure new hardware only.
Rarely. Refurbished options considered only if cost-justified and vendor-proposed.
Sometimes. For some asset classes we evaluate refurbished options and can estimate the embodied savings.
Systematically. Refurbished versus new is part of standard procurement decision with embodied trade-offs analysed.
Circular is default consideration. Refurbished, extended-life, and resale options are actively managed with embodied carbon comparison as standard practice.

domains.name: Carbon intensity and emissions factors
domains.definition: Assesses the quality, precision and governance of emissions factors used to convert activity data into carbon outputs.
domains.why_it_matters: Poor emissions factors can distort otherwise good energy or usage data and create weak location- or time-based decisions.
recommendation_themes: Increase temporal and locational precision where justified

scoring.normalisation: weighted average of selected option scores (1-5), rounded to nearest whole number with .75 thresholds upward
question_override_rules: If more than 30% of answers are level 1, overall maturity cannot exceed 3 unless assessor override is applied.
If evidence coverage is weak across more than half of weighted questions, confidence should be reduced.
If assurance/lineage questions score 1 or 2, decision-readiness should be capped at directional support unless other evidence is strong and override is used.

confidence_logic.low: Sparse evidence, inconsistent notes, or material contradictions across answers.
confidence_logic.medium: Reasonable evidence coverage with some gaps or assumptions.
confidence_logic.high: Strong evidence coverage, consistent answers, clear ownership, documented methods.

questions.text: What is the main basis for the emissions factors you use today?

options.label: Broad annual average factors.
Standard factor sets, but still quite coarse.
A defined set of factors with clear rules across the main areas.
More location- and time-relevant factors where needed.
Decision-grade factors matched to the actual use case.

questions.text: Do the factors reflect the actual location that matters for the decision?

options.label: Rarely.
Only in broad terms.
Usually for the main use cases.
Yes across most relevant decisions.
Yes, with strong location relevance where it matters.

questions.text: How time-sensitive are the factors you use?

options.label: Very broad and static.
Limited time sensitivity.
Good enough for regular reporting and planning.
Good time relevance for most important use cases.
Strong enough for time-sensitive operational decisions where needed.

questions.text: Are location-based, market-based and supplier-specific methods clearly separated and governed?

options.label: No.
Only partly.
Yes for the main reporting use cases.
Yes across most relevant areas.
Yes, clearly governed and consistently applied.

questions.text: Do you have fit-for-purpose factor logic across the estate, or do you rely on broad defaults?

options.label: Mostly broad defaults.
Some fit-for-purpose logic, but not much.
A workable approach across most key areas.
A strong fit-for-purpose approach across the main estate.
Factors are well matched to the decisions they support.

questions.text: How quickly are factor changes picked up in your calculations?

options.label: Very slowly.
With noticeable lag.
In a reasonable timeframe.
Quickly enough for most needs.
Fast enough to stay decision-relevant.

questions.text: Can you explain where the factors came from and why you chose them?

options.label: Not clearly.
Only partly.
The main logic is clear.
The source and rationale are clear across most cases.
The logic is transparent, defensible and well understood.

questions.text: Are the factors good enough for real GreenOps decisions, or just broad reporting?

options.label: Mostly broad reporting only.
Directional use at best.
Good enough for some practical decisions.
Good enough for most key GreenOps decisions.
Clearly strong enough for decision-grade use.

questions.text: Can you distinguish between location-based and market-based carbon accounting? Do you track renewable energy procurement, PPAs, and REC sourcing with appropriate governance?
questions.guidance: Market-based carbon accounting relies on quality of REC sourcing and PPA governance. Location-based versus market-based distinction is essential for credible reporting. Additionality and temporal matching are key quality indicators.
questions.evidence_hint: REC certificates, PPA documentation, renewable energy tracking, carbon accounting methodology documentation

options.label: We do not distinguish between location-based and market-based accounting.
We are aware of the distinction but use only one method without rigorous governance.
We report both location-based and market-based figures. PPA and REC data is collected but governance is informal.
Both methods are applied with clear governance. RECs are tracked, validated, and matched to consumption periods.
Full renewable energy governance including additionality assessment, temporal matching, and auditable PPA/REC documentation integrated into carbon reporting.

1.label: Initial / Ad hoc
1.description: Inputs are fragmented, reactive, estimate-heavy and not reliable enough for confident decision-making.

2.label: Managed / Repeatable
2.description: Inputs can be gathered and reused in a repeatable way, but coverage, precision and attribution remain inconsistent.

3.label: Defined / Standardised
3.description: Data sources, methods and ownership are documented and standardised across key areas.

4.label: Quantitatively Managed / Measured
4.description: Data quality, granularity, coverage and confidence are measured and controlled using evidence.

5.label: Optimising
5.description: Inputs are continuously improved, increasingly automated and embedded into operational and strategic decision-making.

supports: Broad awareness and high-level reporting for carbon intensity and emissions factors

does_not_support: Confident automation or tight target accountability

supports: Some controlled decisions in priority areas
does_not_support: Strong claims of precision across the whole estate

supports: Strong management, engineering and governance use
does_not_support: None by design; caveats should still be made explicit where relevant

recommendation_triggers.guidance: Prioritise foundational improvement in carbon intensity and emissions factors before making strong decision-grade claims.
Standardise and expand carbon intensity and emissions factors so it supports more controlled decisions.
Use stronger carbon intensity and emissions factors capability to drive optimisation and measurable intervention, not just reporting.

domains.name: Asset, workload, application and service usage maturity
domains.definition: Assesses whether the organisation understands whether infrastructure and services are being used efficiently and meaningfully, including VM-, app-, service- and AI-relevant usage signals.
domains.why_it_matters: Without utilisation and service usage visibility, GreenOps cannot move from reporting to avoiding low-value demand and optimising live services.
recommendation_themes: Link usage to services and owners
Include AI prompts/tokens/inference metrics where relevant

scoring.normalisation: weighted average of selected option scores (1-5), rounded to nearest whole number with .75 thresholds upward
question_override_rules: If more than 30% of answers are level 1, overall maturity cannot exceed 3 unless assessor override is applied.
If evidence coverage is weak across more than half of weighted questions, confidence should be reduced.
If assurance/lineage questions score 1 or 2, decision-readiness should be capped at directional support unless other evidence is strong and override is used.

confidence_logic.low: Sparse evidence, inconsistent notes, or material contradictions across answers.
confidence_logic.medium: Reasonable evidence coverage with some gaps or assumptions.
confidence_logic.high: Strong evidence coverage, consistent answers, clear ownership, documented methods.

questions.text: How much visibility do you have into how infrastructure is actually being used?

options.label: Very little.
Some broad visibility only.
A workable view across the main platforms.
Good visibility across most relevant environments.
Strong, trusted visibility that supports optimisation.

questions.text: How well do you measure real application or service usage?

options.label: Hardly at all.
Only for some services.
We can measure usage for many important services.
We have good service usage visibility across most main areas.
We measure service usage well enough to drive real decisions.

questions.text: Can you link usage back to a named service, owner, product, user group or business purpose?

options.label: Rarely.
Only in some cases.
For many important services or platforms.
For most relevant services and workloads.
Yes, clearly and consistently.

questions.text: How well do you spot idle, oversized, dormant or low-value consumption?

options.label: We mostly do not.
We notice some obvious cases.
We can identify many of the main issues.
We detect them well across most relevant areas.
We identify and challenge them as part of normal operations.

questions.text: How often can you see changes in usage patterns?

options.label: Rarely.
Only periodically.
Often enough for baseline management.
Frequently enough for regular optimisation.
Quickly enough to support active demand management.

questions.text: Can you compare usage properly across environments, regions or hosting types?

options.label: Not really.
Only in broad terms.
Yes for many major areas.
Yes across most of the main estate.
Yes, in a way that supports real comparison and decision-making.

questions.text: If you run AI services, how well do you measure things like prompts, tokens, inference calls, training jobs and model use?

options.label: We do not track this meaningfully.
We have a few basic figures, but they are incomplete.
We can see core AI demand measures for the main services.
We have good AI usage visibility and can compare services and use cases.
We have strong AI usage data that supports governance and optimisation.

questions.text: Can you explain where the usage data came from and how much confidence you have in it?

options.label: Not clearly.
Only partly.
The main sources are reasonably clear.
Source and confidence are clear across most relevant areas.
The data is transparent, trusted and fit for decision use.

questions.text: Do you track and govern resource consumption for AI/ML workloads including training jobs, fine-tuning, and batch inference?
questions.guidance: Training jobs and fine-tuning consume significant GPU resources. Governance means tracking who initiates jobs, what they use, expected business value, and cost/carbon approval gates.
questions.evidence_hint: ML training job logs, approval system records, cost allocation for GPU infrastructure

options.label: No governance. Training jobs run ad-hoc without tracking or approval.
We can see some training jobs in logs but no centralised tracking or approval process.
We track major training jobs (team, dataset size, GPU hours, date). Informally reviewed by ML lead.
Formal tracking system with approval gate for jobs above a cost threshold. Most training jobs are logged and reviewed.
Full governance: approval gate covering cost and carbon, job tracking dashboard with resource and outcome analysis, post-mortem reviews on expensive jobs.

questions.text: For cloud-hosted AI services such as LLM APIs, can you track usage volumes and associated carbon impact?
questions.guidance: Many organisations use cloud AI APIs (GPT-4, Claude, etc). Usage is measurable via tokens and inference calls. Major cloud providers and AI vendors increasingly publish carbon intensity data.
questions.evidence_hint: Cloud AI service usage logs, API call tracking, cost allocation by service, carbon intensity estimates

options.label: We use cloud AI services but do not track usage or cost at service level.
We can see aggregate AI spend in cloud bills but no usage-level granularity.
We measure usage (tokens, requests) for the main AI services. Not yet integrated into carbon accounting.
We track AI usage by service and team with estimated carbon per service based on provider data.
Granular AI usage tracking linked to carbon estimates from vendor data, integrated into service-level carbon dashboards.

questions.text: Do you systematically optimise AI workloads for computational efficiency — for example through prompt engineering, model quantisation, inference caching, or batch processing?
questions.guidance: AI workloads can be 10x more efficient with targeted optimisation. Prompt engineering (shorter context), model quantisation (faster/cheaper), inference caching (avoid re-inference), and batch processing all reduce resource consumption.
questions.evidence_hint: Prompt design guidelines, model selection criteria, quantisation policies, inference cost tracking

options.label: No. We optimise for accuracy and capability, not efficiency.
Occasionally. Individual engineers optimise prompts or select smaller models.
Yes for some critical services. Cost and efficiency are considered in AI system design.
Systematically. Prompt engineering, model sizing, and quantisation choices are part of standard practice.
Efficiency is a first-class requirement. Automated testing for efficiency, with carbon and cost per inference tracked and optimised.

domains.name: Allocation and attribution logic
domains.definition: Assesses whether environmental impact can be credibly assigned to teams, services, workloads, products or consumers.
domains.why_it_matters: Without attribution, outputs remain interesting but hard to act on. Attribution is where reporting becomes operationally useful.
recommendation_themes: Move from broad averages to usage-aware attribution
Link impacts to owners and services

scoring.normalisation: weighted average of selected option scores (1-5), rounded to nearest whole number with .75 thresholds upward
question_override_rules: If more than 30% of answers are level 1, overall maturity cannot exceed 3 unless assessor override is applied.
If evidence coverage is weak across more than half of weighted questions, confidence should be reduced.
If assurance/lineage questions score 1 or 2, decision-readiness should be capped at directional support unless other evidence is strong and override is used.

confidence_logic.low: Sparse evidence, inconsistent notes, or material contradictions across answers.
confidence_logic.medium: Reasonable evidence coverage with some gaps or assumptions.
confidence_logic.high: Strong evidence coverage, consistent answers, clear ownership, documented methods.

questions.text: How detailed is your current attribution of environmental impact?

options.label: Most impact is still at total-estate level.
We can split some impact, but only in broad terms.
We can attribute most major areas to known services, teams or environments.
We have good operational attribution across much of the estate.
We have strong, decision-grade attribution that supports accountability and action.

questions.text: How credible is the method you use to attribute impact?

options.label: Low credibility.
Basic and assumption-heavy.
Reasonable for the main use cases.
Strong across most relevant decisions.
Robust and trusted enough for real accountability.

questions.text: How much of the footprint can you attribute meaningfully rather than leaving at portfolio level?

options.label: Very little.
Some areas only.
A good portion of the main footprint.
Most key areas can be attributed usefully.
Attribution coverage is strong and operationally useful.

questions.text: Can you link attributed impact back to named owners, products or teams?

options.label: Rarely.
Only in some areas.
For many main services or teams.
For most relevant owners and services.
Yes, clearly and consistently.

questions.text: Does the attribution reflect actual usage and demand, or mostly static allocation rules?

options.label: Mostly static rules.
Some usage input, but still mainly broad allocation.
A workable mix of real usage and allocation logic.
Good use of actual demand across most relevant areas.
Strong demand-based attribution where it matters.

questions.text: Where location changes impact materially, does your attribution reflect that?

options.label: No.
Only loosely.
Broadly yes for main use cases.
Yes across most relevant decisions.
Yes, with strong locational accuracy where it matters.

questions.text: Can you explain the attribution method clearly to stakeholders?

options.label: Not really.
Only at a high level.
Yes for the main use cases.
Yes, clearly across most relevant areas.
Yes, in a transparent and defensible way.

questions.text: Is the attribution good enough to drive real decisions and accountability?

options.label: No, not yet.
Only at a broad directional level.
Good enough for some practical decisions.
Good enough for most key operational and management decisions.
Clearly strong enough for decision-grade use and accountability.

questions.text: Are your carbon attribution and cost allocation methods aligned — using the same cost drivers and allocation keys?
questions.guidance: Carbon and cost should be allocated together using the same drivers (CPU hours, storage GB, etc). Misalignment creates confusion and blocks operational action. This alignment is essential for FinOps-GreenOps integration.
questions.evidence_hint: Cost allocation model documentation, carbon allocation model, FinOps and GreenOps governance alignment

options.label: Carbon and cost use entirely different allocation methods with no alignment.
Only loosely aligned. Some cost drivers map to carbon drivers but many do not.
Mostly aligned. Main cost drivers (compute, storage, bandwidth) map to carbon allocation drivers.
Fully aligned. Carbon and cost use identical allocation drivers. FinOps and GreenOps teams coordinate.
Integrated dashboards where carbon and cost per service are co-reported. Cost-carbon trade-offs are explicit in decisions.

questions.text: Can you link cloud telemetry back to tags, owners, products, services or business units?

questions.text: How often is cloud telemetry available at a useful level?

options.label: Infrequently.
Periodically, but not very useful operationally.
Often enough for regular analysis.

Timely enough for active operational use.

questions.text: Does the telemetry reflect the actual cloud region, zone or environment where it matters?

Only at broad level.

Fast enough for regular management.

Only partly.

Source and confidence are clear across most relevant areas.
The telemetry is transparent, trusted and fit for decision use.

questions.text: Do you use cloud provider carbon intensity data to inform workload placement decisions — choosing lower-carbon regions or availability zones where practical?
questions.guidance: Major cloud providers publish carbon intensity by region. Using this data in workload placement decisions is a practical lever for reducing operational carbon, particularly for flexible workloads.
questions.evidence_hint: Cloud provider sustainability dashboards, region selection criteria, workload placement policies

options.label: We do not consider carbon intensity in workload placement decisions.
We are aware that regions differ in carbon intensity but it does not influence placement.
We consider carbon intensity for some new deployments but existing workloads are not reviewed.
Carbon intensity is a factor in workload placement policy. Major workloads are reviewed for region optimisation.
Carbon-aware placement is standard. Flexible workloads are scheduled to lower-carbon regions and times. Real-time carbon signals inform dynamic placement.

options.label: No, or hardly at all.
Only partly.
Yes in most key areas.
Yes, clearly and consistently in the main outputs.
Yes, explicitly and in a way stakeholders can rely on.

questions.text: Is the overall assurance level strong enough for the stakeholders using the outputs?

options.label: No.
Only for limited use.
Good enough for baseline reporting and some decisions.
Good enough for most intended stakeholders and uses.
Strong enough for decision-grade and governance use.

1.label: Initial / Ad hoc
1.description: Inputs are fragmented, reactive, estimate-heavy and not reliable enough for confident decision-making.

2.label: Managed / Repeatable
2.description: Inputs can be gathered and reused in a repeatable way, but coverage, precision and attribution remain inconsistent.

3.label: Defined / Standardised
3.description: Data sources, methods and ownership are documented and standardised across key areas.

4.label: Quantitatively Managed / Measured
4.description: Data quality, granularity, coverage and confidence are measured and controlled using evidence.

5.label: Optimising
5.description: Inputs are continuously improved, increasingly automated and embedded into operational and strategic decision-making.

supports: Broad awareness and high-level reporting for data lineage, transparency and assurance

does_not_support: Confident automation or tight target accountability

supports: Some controlled decisions in priority areas
does_not_support: Strong claims of precision across the whole estate

supports: Strong management, engineering and governance use
does_not_support: None by design; caveats should still be made explicit where relevant

recommendation_triggers.guidance: Prioritise foundational improvement in data lineage, transparency and assurance before making strong decision-grade claims.
Standardise and expand data lineage, transparency and assurance so it supports more controlled decisions.
Use stronger data lineage, transparency and assurance capability to drive optimisation and measurable intervention, not just reporting.

domains.name: Decision integration and optimisation workflow
domains.definition: Assesses whether GreenOps inputs are actually embedded into planning, engineering, governance and optimisation processes.
domains.why_it_matters: GreenOps maturity is not just about good data. It is about using the data to change decisions and outcomes.
recommendation_themes: Embed GreenOps into existing decision forums
Tie insights to owners and workflow

scoring.normalisation: weighted average of selected option scores (1-5), rounded to nearest whole number with .75 thresholds upward
question_override_rules: If more than 30% of answers are level 1, overall maturity cannot exceed 3 unless assessor override is applied.
If evidence coverage is weak across more than half of weighted questions, confidence should be reduced.
If assurance/lineage questions score 1 or 2, decision-readiness should be capped at directional support unless other evidence is strong and override is used.

confidence_logic.low: Sparse evidence, inconsistent notes, or material contradictions across answers.
confidence_logic.medium: Reasonable evidence coverage with some gaps or assumptions.
confidence_logic.high: Strong evidence coverage, consistent answers, clear ownership, documented methods.

questions.text: How often is GreenOps input used in real decisions rather than just reporting?

options.label: Mainly for reporting or presentations.
It occasionally informs one-off decisions.
It is used in some regular decision processes.
It is used consistently in key operational and planning decisions.
It is embedded in normal management, optimisation and governance.

questions.text: Can you link GreenOps insights to named owners and actions?

options.label: Rarely.
Only in some cases.
For many important actions.
For most relevant actions and decisions.
Yes, clearly and consistently.

questions.text: How mature is the workflow for turning insight into optimisation activity?

options.label: Very weak or ad hoc.
Basic and inconsistent.
A workable process exists for the main areas.
A strong repeatable workflow exists across most relevant areas.
Optimisation is part of normal operating practice.

questions.text: How much of the estate or organisation is covered by this decision process?

options.label: Very little.
Some areas only.
A good portion of the main estate.
Most relevant areas are covered.
Coverage is broad and operationally meaningful.

questions.text: How quickly can teams act on GreenOps signals?

options.label: Slowly, if at all.
With noticeable delay.
In a reasonable timeframe.
Quickly enough for most operational needs.
Fast enough to support continuous improvement.

questions.text: Can current workflows support time- or location-sensitive decisions where needed?

options.label: No.
Only in a very limited way.
Broadly yes for some key use cases.
Yes for most relevant decisions.
Yes, in a way that supports real operational optimisation.

questions.text: Do you measure whether actions actually delivered the expected outcome?

options.label: Rarely.
Only in some cases.
Broadly yes for major actions.
Yes across most important actions.
Yes, consistently and in a way that improves future decisions.

questions.text: Is it fair to say your GreenOps data is truly decision-grade today?

options.label: No.
Only in a few limited areas.
In some important areas, yes.
Across most key decisions, yes.
Yes, clearly and consistently.

1.label: Initial / Ad hoc
1.description: Inputs are fragmented, reactive, estimate-heavy and not reliable enough for confident decision-making.

2.label: Managed / Repeatable
2.description: Inputs can be gathered and reused in a repeatable way, but coverage, precision and attribution remain inconsistent.

3.label: Defined / Standardised
3.description: Data sources, methods and ownership are documented and standardised across key areas.

4.label: Quantitatively Managed / Measured
4.description: Data quality, granularity, coverage and confidence are measured and controlled using evidence.

5.label: Optimising
5.description: Inputs are continuously improved, increasingly automated and embedded into operational and strategic decision-making.

does_not_support: Confident automation or tight target accountability

supports: Some controlled decisions in priority areas
does_not_support: Strong claims of precision across the whole estate

supports: Strong management, engineering and governance use
does_not_support: None by design; caveats should still be made explicit where relevant

Standardise and expand decision integration and optimisation workflow so it supports more controlled decisions.
Use stronger decision integration and optimisation workflow capability to drive optimisation and measurable intervention, not just reporting.
